title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: Null hypothesis significance testing (NHST) dominant paradigm biological research decades, yet limitations discussed 77 years. core issue p-value answers narrow question — “effect non-zero?” — telling us nothing large effect precise estimate . Ho et al. (2019) put : Although NHST limits analyst ill-conceived question “?”, estimation instead draws analyst’s attention question “much?” — topic defines quantitative research. Estimation statistics replaces dichotomous significant/non-significant thinking focus : Effect sizes: large difference? (mean difference, Cohen’s d, Cliff’s delta, correlation coefficient, regression slope…) Bootstrap confidence intervals: precise estimate? range plausible values? framework, championed Gardner & Altman (1986) formalized DABEST software (Ho et al. 2019; estimationstats.com), produces estimation plots show individual data points alongside effect size uncertainty. gives much richer picture single p-value: data visible: Every observation plotted, exposing distribution shape, outliers, sample size glance. Effect size front center: magnitude difference displayed directly, hidden behind test statistic. Uncertainty explicit: Bootstrap confidence intervals convey precision, enabling readers judge whether effect meaningful practice. Reproducibility: Effect sizes CIs travel well across studies facilitate meta-analysis, unlike p-values depend sample size.

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: functions comparpq report p-values (permutation test, Welch’s t-test, Mann-Whitney U, cor.test) alongside estimation statistics. provided legacy purposes , satisfy journals still require . Following recommendations Ho et al. (2019), encourage users : Never use p-values primary basis interpretation. Focus effect size confidence interval. State Methods: “null-hypothesis significance testing performed; p-values provided legacy purposes .” thorough introduction estimation statistics, see estimationstats.com/background.

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: Gardner-Altman plot (2 groups): raw data effect size bootstrap CI displayed side side shared axis, making comparison immediately visual. Cumming plot (3+ groups): groups shown top, effect sizes CIs shown separate panel. layout scales multiple comparisons. comparpq provides four functions estimation statistics phyloseq objects:

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
code: library(comparpq) library(phyloseq) library(ggplot2) library(dplyr) data("data_fungi", package = "MiscMetabar")  # Clean data: remove samples with NA in Height pq <- subset_samples(data_fungi, !is.na(Height)) pq <- clean_pq(pq)

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: two groups, estim_diff_pq() produces Gardner-Altman plots raw data effect size displayed side side:
code: res <- estim_diff_pq(pq, fact = "Height")  # View summary table res$summary  # View the Gardner-Altman plot for Hill_0 (richness) res$plots$Hill_0

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: can choose among several effect size measures:
code: # Cohen's d (standardized mean difference) res_d <- estim_diff_pq(pq, fact = "Height", effect_type = "cohens_d") res_d$summary  # Hedges' g (bias-corrected Cohen's d for small samples) res_g <- estim_diff_pq(pq, fact = "Height", effect_type = "hedges_g") res_g$summary  # Cliff's delta (non-parametric effect size) res_cliff <- estim_diff_pq(pq, fact = "Height", effect_type = "cliffs_delta") res_cliff$summary

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: factor 3+ levels, Cumming estimation plot produced automatically:
code: # If your factor has 3+ levels pq_time <- subset_samples(data_fungi, !is.na(Time)) pq_time <- clean_pq(pq_time)  res_time <- estim_diff_pq(pq_time, fact = "Time") res_time$plots$Hill_0

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: continuous variables, estim_cor_pq() computes bootstrap confidence intervals correlation coefficients regression slopes:
code: # Add library size as a numeric variable sam <- sample_data(pq) sam$lib_size <- sample_sums(pq) sample_data(pq) <- sam  res_cor <- estim_cor_pq(pq, variable = "lib_size")  # Correlation estimates with bootstrap CIs res_cor$correlations  # Regression slopes with bootstrap CIs res_cor$regressions  # Scatter plot with regression line and CI ribbon res_cor$plots$Hill_0

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
code: res_spearman <- estim_cor_pq(pq, variable = "lib_size", method = "spearman") res_spearman$correlations

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: Apply estimation statistics across multiple phyloseq objects list_phyloseq:
code: lpq <- list_phyloseq(   list(     fungi = pq,     fungi_copy = pq   ),   same_bioinfo_pipeline = FALSE )  res_lpq <- estim_diff_lpq(lpq, fact = "Height")  # Combined summary across all pipelines res_lpq$summary  # Access individual results res_lpq$results$fungi$plots$Hill_0

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
code: # Assuming lib_size is in sample_data of all phyloseq objects res_cor_lpq <- estim_cor_lpq(lpq, variable = "lib_size")  # Compare correlations across pipelines res_cor_lpq$correlations  # Compare regression slopes res_cor_lpq$regressions

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: can supply custom diversity function instead Hill numbers. function must take phyloseq object return either: named numeric vector (names = sample names) data.frame one row per sample
code: # Example: use Pielou's evenness my_evenness <- function(physeq) {   otu <- as.data.frame(otu_table(physeq))   if (!taxa_are_rows(physeq)) otu <- t(otu)   otu <- t(otu)   H <- vegan::diversity(otu, index = "shannon")   S <- vegan::specnumber(otu)   J <- H / log(S)   stats::setNames(J, sample_names(physeq)) }  res_even <- estim_diff_pq(pq, fact = "Height", custom_fn = my_evenness) res_even$summary

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: Gardner, M. J., & Altman, D. G. (1986). Confidence intervals rather P values: estimation rather hypothesis testing. BMJ, 292(6522), 746-750. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.292.6522.746 Ho, J., Tumkaya, T., Aryal, S., Choi, H., & Claridge-Chang, . (2019). Moving beyond P values: data analysis estimation graphics. Nature Methods, 16(7), 565-566. https://www.nature.com/articles/s41592-019-0470-3 Claridge-Chang, ., & Assam, P. N. (2016). Estimation statistics replace significance testing. Nature Methods, 13(2), 108-109.

title: Estimation Statistics for Microbiome Data
text: estimationstats.com — Interactive web application estimation statistics background tutorial, free dataset upload, automated estimation plots. dabestr R package — R implementation DABEST used internally comparpq.

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: comparpq extension package {MiscMetabar} {phyloseq} designed comparing phyloseq objects. package particularly useful : Evaluating taxonomic assignment accuracy using mock communities Creating interactive visualizations microbiome data Comparing multiple taxonomic databases assignment methods Data preprocessing quality control

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: can install comparpq GitHub:
code: # Install from GitHub if (!requireNamespace("devtools", quietly = TRUE)) {   install.packages("devtools") } devtools::install_github("adrientaudiere/comparpq")

title: Getting Started with comparpq
code: library(comparpq)  # The package automatically loads dependencies including: # - phyloseq for handling microbiome data # - MiscMetabar for additional microbiome analysis tools

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: package includes Glom_otu dataset, contains glomalean (arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi) OTU data:
code:
Glom_otu #> phyloseq-class experiment-level object #> otu_table()   OTU Table:         [ 1147 taxa and 444 samples ] #> sample_data() Sample Data:       [ 444 samples by 118 sample variables ] #> tax_table()   Taxonomy Table:    [ 1147 taxa by 14 taxonomic ranks ] #> refseq()      DNAStringSet:      [ 1147 reference sequences ] # Basic information about the dataset cat("Number of taxa:", phyloseq::ntaxa(Glom_otu), "
") #> Number of taxa: 1147 cat("Number of samples:", phyloseq::nsamples(Glom_otu), "
") #> Number of samples: 444 cat("Taxonomic ranks:", paste(phyloseq::rank_names(Glom_otu), collapse = ", "), "
") #> Taxonomic ranks: Kingdom, Phyla, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species, Kingdom__eukaryome_Glomero, Phylum__eukaryome_Glomero, Class__eukaryome_Glomero, Order__eukaryome_Glomero, Family__eukaryome_Glomero, Genus__eukaryome_Glomero, Species__eukaryome_Glomero

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: One key features comparpq ability create interactive bubble plots using Observable HQ:
code: # Basic bubble plot bubbles_pq(Glom_otu) # Bubble plot colored by Family bubbles_pq(Glom_otu,   rank_color = "Family",   min_nb_seq = 1000 ) # Customized bubble plot with different color scheme bubbles_pq(Glom_otu,   rank_color = "Class",   categorical_scheme = "d3.schemePastel1",   min_nb_seq = 500,   log1ptransform = TRUE )

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: non-interactive visualizations, can use:
code: # Create comparison matrices for visualization matrix_data <- tc_points_matrix(Glom_otu)  # Bar chart comparison (requires appropriate data structure) # tc_bar(matrix_data)  # Circular comparison plot # tc_circle(matrix_data)

title: Getting Started with comparpq
code:
# Select specific taxonomic ranks Glom_otu_subset <- select_ranks_pq(Glom_otu, Kingdom, Genus) cat("Original ranks:", paste(phyloseq::rank_names(Glom_otu), collapse = ", "), "
") #> Original ranks: Kingdom, Phyla, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species, Kingdom__eukaryome_Glomero, Phylum__eukaryome_Glomero, Class__eukaryome_Glomero, Order__eukaryome_Glomero, Family__eukaryome_Glomero, Genus__eukaryome_Glomero, Species__eukaryome_Glomero cat("Selected ranks:", paste(phyloseq::rank_names(Glom_otu_subset), collapse = ", "), "
") #> Selected ranks: Kingdom, Genus # Rename taxonomic ranks renamed_physeq <- rename_ranks_pq(Glom_otu,   new_names = c(     "Domain", "Division", "Class_level",     "Order_level", "Family_level", "Genus_level"   ) ) cat("Renamed ranks:", paste(phyloseq::rank_names(renamed_physeq), collapse = ", "), "
") #> Renamed ranks: Kingdom, Phyla, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species, Kingdom__eukaryome_Glomero, Phylum__eukaryome_Glomero, Class__eukaryome_Glomero, Order__eukaryome_Glomero, Family__eukaryome_Glomero, Genus__eukaryome_Glomero, Species__eukaryome_Glomero

title: Getting Started with comparpq
code: # Replace unwanted patterns with NA # This is useful for cleaning taxonomic assignments cleaned_physeq <- taxtab_replace_pattern_by_NA(Glom_otu,   pattern = "^uncultured",   taxonomic_ranks = "Genus" )  # Check how many taxa were affected original_genus <- phyloseq::tax_table(Glom_otu)[, "Genus"] cleaned_genus <- phyloseq::tax_table(cleaned_physeq)[, "Genus"] cat(   "Uncultured genera replaced with NA:",   sum(is.na(cleaned_genus)) - sum(is.na(original_genus)), "\n" ) #> Uncultured genera replaced with NA: 0

what: Creating Fake Taxa for Accuracy Assessment
title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: evaluate taxonomic assignment accuracy, can add fake taxa dataset:
code:
# Add shuffled sequences (creates fake taxa from existing sequences) physeq_with_shuffled <- add_shuffle_seq_pq(Glom_otu, n_fake = 10) cat("Original taxa count:", phyloseq::ntaxa(Glom_otu), "
") #> Original taxa count: 1147 cat("With fake taxa:", phyloseq::ntaxa(physeq_with_shuffled), "
") #> With fake taxa: 1157  # Add external sequences (simulates contamination or database gaps) external_seq <- Biostrings::readDNAStringSet(   system.file("extdata/ex_little.fasta", package = "MiscMetabar") ) physeq_with_external <- add_external_seq_pq(Glom_otu, ext_seqs = external_seq) cat("With external sequences added:", phyloseq::ntaxa(physeq_with_external), "
") #> With external sequences added: 1149

title: Getting Started with comparpq
code: # Example of computing accuracy metrics # This requires properly formatted comparison data  # Step 1: Prepare your comparison data structure # ranks_df should contain your taxonomic assignments to evaluate # true_values_df should contain the known correct assignments  # Example structure (replace with your actual data): # ranks_df <- data.frame( #   method1 = c("Fungi", "Ascomycota", "Sordariomycetes"), #   method2 = c("Fungi", "Basidiomycota", "Agaricomycetes") # ) # # true_values_df <- data.frame( #   Kingdom = c("Fungi", "Fungi"), #   Phylum = c("Glomeromycota", "Glomeromycota") # )  # Step 2: Compute metrics # accuracy_results <- tc_metrics_mock(physeq_with_shuffled, #                                   ranks_df = ranks_df, #                                   true_values_df = true_values_df, #                                   fake_taxa = TRUE)

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: package includes specialized visualization understanding missing taxonomic data:
code: # Visualize NA patterns in taxonomic assignments rainplot_taxo_na(Glom_otu)

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: working multiple taxonomic databases, conflicts may arise:
code: # Resolve taxonomic conflicts between different assignment methods # This function helps when you have conflicting taxonomic assignments resolved_physeq <- resolve_taxo_conflict(Glom_otu,   conflict_rank = "Genus",   resolve_method = "majority" )

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: Prepare data: Start phyloseq object containing OTU/ASV data Add fake taxa: Use add_shuffle_seq_pq() add_external_seq_pq() create test cases Perform taxonomic assignments: Apply different methods/databases data Compare results: Use tc_metrics_mock() quantify accuracy Visualize: Create plots using bubbles_pq(), tc_bar(), tc_circle()

title: Getting Started with comparpq
code: # Example quality control workflow qc_physeq <- Glom_otu  # 1. Clean up problematic annotations qc_physeq <- taxtab_replace_pattern_by_NA(qc_physeq,   pattern = "unknown|unidentified|uncultured",   taxonomic_ranks = c("Family", "Genus") )  # 2. Select relevant taxonomic ranks qc_physeq <- select_ranks_pq(qc_physeq,   taxonomic_ranks = c("Kingdom", "Phyla", "Class", "Order", "Family") )  # 3. Standardize rank names qc_physeq <- rename_ranks_pq(qc_physeq,   old_names = c("Kingdom", "Phyla", "Class", "Order", "Family"),   new_names = c("Kingdom__", "Phyla__", "Class__", "Order__", "Family__") )  cat("Quality control completed. Final dataset has", phyloseq::ntaxa(qc_physeq), "taxa\n") #> Quality control completed. Final dataset has 1147 taxa

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: Function documentation: Use ?function_name detailed help function Package website: Visit https://adrientaudiere.github.io/comparpq/ Report issues: https://github.com/adrientaudiere/comparpq/issues {MiscMetabar}: Extended microbiome analysis {phyloseq}: Core microbiome data handling

title: Getting Started with comparpq
text: comparpq package provides comprehensive toolkit : Interactive visualization phyloseq objects using modern web technologies Accuracy assessment taxonomic assignment methods using mock communities Data preprocessing quality control microbiome analyses Comparative analysis different taxonomic databases assignment approaches makes particularly valuable researchers working taxonomic assignment method development, database comparison, microbiome data quality assessment.

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: list_phyloseq class S7 class designed store compare multiple phyloseq objects. automatically computes summary statistics phyloseq object determines type comparison based : Detected characteristics: sample overlap, nested samples, shared modalities User-provided parameters: same_primer_seq_tech same_bioinfo_pipeline vignette demonstrates: create list_phyloseq objects appropriate parameters six types comparisons use Utility functions manipulating list_phyloseq objects Visualization functions exploring presenting results

title: The list_phyloseq Class
code: devtools::load_all() # library(comparpq) library(phyloseq)

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: simplest way create list_phyloseq pass named list phyloseq objects. can also specify same_primer_seq_tech same_bioinfo_pipeline parameters control comparison type: output shows: Summary table: Statistics phyloseq object (n_samples, n_taxa, n_sequences, etc.) Comparison characteristics: Type comparison, same_primer_seq_tech, same_bioinfo_pipeline, sample overlap, shared modalities
code: lpq <- list_phyloseq(list(   fungi = data_fungi,   fungi_mini = data_fungi_mini ))  lpq #> list_phyloseq object with 2 phyloseq objects #>  #> --- Summary --- #> # A tibble: 2 × 17 #>   name   n_samples n_taxa n_sequences n_occurence mean_seq_length min_seq_length #>   <chr>      <int>  <int>       <dbl>       <int>           <dbl>          <int> #> 1 fungi        185   1420     1839124       12499            318.            251 #> 2 fungi…       137     45      569525         560            343.            303 #> # ℹ 10 more variables: mean_seq_per_sample <dbl>, sd_seq_per_sample <dbl>, #> #   min_seq_per_sample <dbl>, max_seq_per_sample <dbl>, #> #   mean_seq_per_taxon <dbl>, sd_seq_per_taxon <dbl>, has_sam_data <lgl>, #> #   has_tax_table <lgl>, has_refseq <lgl>, has_phy_tree <lgl> #>  #> --- Comparison characteristics --- #> Type of comparison: NESTED_ROBUSTNESS  #> Nested samples detected (one phyloseq derived from another). #>   Nesting: fungi_mini (137 samples) is nested in fungi (185 samples) #>   Useful to test robustness to data processing (e.g., rarefaction). #>   Comparisons should focus on the common (nested) samples.  #>  #> Same primer/seq tech: TRUE  #> Same bioinfo pipeline: TRUE  #> Same sample_data structure: TRUE  #> Same samples: FALSE  #> Nested samples: TRUE  #> Same taxa: FALSE  #> Common samples: 137  #> Common taxa: 45  #>  #> --- Reference sequence comparison --- #> fungi_vs_fungi_mini: 45 shared seqs, 1375 unique in fungi, 0 unique in fungi_mini

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: creating list_phyloseq, can specify: same_primer_seq_tech (logical, default TRUE): Set FALSE comparing different primers (e.g., ITS1 vs ITS2) technologies (e.g., Illumina vs PacBio) same_bioinfo_pipeline (logical, default TRUE): Set FALSE comparing different clustering methods, taxonomic databases, analysis parameters

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: list_phyloseq class determines type comparison based detected sample overlap user-provided same_primer_seq_tech same_bioinfo_pipeline parameters. six main types:

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: Type | Samples | primer/seq_tech | pipeline | pipeline modality | Use Case | modality | Use Case | |——|————–|———————-|———————–|———-||———-| | REPRODUCIBILITY | Yes | TRUE | TRUE | TRUE | Test reproducibility identical analysis | | ROBUSTNESS | Yes | TRUE | FALSE | TRUE | Test effect different pipelines/parameters | | REPLICABILITY | Yes | FALSE | - | TRUE | Test effect different primers/technologies | | NESTED_ROBUSTNESS | Nested | - | - | TRUE | Test effect rarefaction/filtering | | EXPLORATION | | - | - | TRUE | Explore differences sample groups | | SEPARATE_ANALYSIS | | - | - | FALSE | Independent analyses (comparison may meaningful) |

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: phyloseq objects samples, primer/technology (same_primer_seq_tech = TRUE), pipeline (same_bioinfo_pipeline = TRUE, defaults), comparison classified REPRODUCIBILITY. can also useful test effect stochasticity function.
code:
# REPRODUCIBILITY: Same samples, same pipeline (default parameters) # Example: Running the same analysis twice to test reproducibility lpq_repro <- list_phyloseq(list(   run1 = data_fungi,   run2 = data_fungi ))  cat("Type:", lpq_repro@comparison$type_of_comparison, "
") #> Type: REPRODUCIBILITY cat("Same primer/seq tech:", lpq_repro@comparison$same_primer_seq_tech, "
") #> Same primer/seq tech: TRUE cat("Same bioinfo pipeline:", lpq_repro@comparison$same_bioinfo_pipeline, "
") #> Same bioinfo pipeline: TRUE # REPRODUCIBILITY: Same samples, same pipeline (default parameters) # Example: Running the same analysis twice to test reproducibility lpq_repro <- list_phyloseq(list(   run1 = rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi, rngseed = 66),   run2 = rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi, rngseed = 42) ))  cat("Type:", lpq_repro@comparison$type_of_comparison, "
") #> Type: REPRODUCIBILITY cat("Same primer/seq tech:", lpq_repro@comparison$same_primer_seq_tech, "
") #> Same primer/seq tech: TRUE cat("Same bioinfo pipeline:", lpq_repro@comparison$same_bioinfo_pipeline, "
") #> Same bioinfo pipeline: TRUE

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: phyloseq objects samples primer/technology, different pipelines (same_bioinfo_pipeline = FALSE), comparison classified ROBUSTNESS.
code:
# ROBUSTNESS: Same samples, different pipeline # Example: Comparing different clustering methods or taxonomic databases lpq_robust <- list_phyloseq(   list(     raw_fungi = data_fungi,     fungi_swarm = postcluster_pq(data_fungi, method = "swarm"),     fungi_mumu = mumu_pq(data_fungi)$new_physeq   ),   same_bioinfo_pipeline = FALSE # Different bioinformatics pipelines )  cat("Type:", lpq_robust@comparison$type_of_comparison, "
") #> Type: ROBUSTNESS cat("Same primer/seq tech:", lpq_robust@comparison$same_primer_seq_tech, "
") #> Same primer/seq tech: TRUE cat("Same bioinfo pipeline:", lpq_robust@comparison$same_bioinfo_pipeline, "
") #> Same bioinfo pipeline: FALSE

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: phyloseq objects samples different primers technologies (same_primer_seq_tech = FALSE), comparison classified REPLICABILITY.
code: # REPLICABILITY: Same samples, different primer/technology # Example: Comparing ITS1 vs ITS2, or Illumina vs PacBio lpq_replic <- list_phyloseq(   list(     ITS1 = data_fungi,     ITS2 = data_fungi # In real use, this would be data from a different primer   ),   same_primer_seq_tech = FALSE # Different primers/technologies )  cat("Type:", lpq_replic@comparison$type_of_comparison, "\n") #> Type: REPLICABILITY cat("Same primer/seq tech:", lpq_replic@comparison$same_primer_seq_tech, "\n") #> Same primer/seq tech: FALSE

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: one phyloseq object derived another (e.g., rarefied version), samples one subset samples another. type detected automatically regardless same_primer_seq_tech same_bioinfo_pipeline parameters. useful testing robustness data processing choices like rarefaction filtering. comparison focus common (nested) samples make meaningful comparisons. Use filter_common_lpq() (see Utility Functions).
code:
# Create a rarefied version (samples may be lost if they don't meet depth threshold) set.seed(123) data_fungi_rarefied <- rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi, sample.size = 1000, verbose = FALSE)  lpq_nested <- list_phyloseq(list(   original = data_fungi,   rarefied = data_fungi_rarefied ))  # Check the comparison type cat("Type:", lpq_nested@comparison$type_of_comparison, "
") #> Type: NESTED_ROBUSTNESS cat("Nested samples:", lpq_nested@comparison$nested_samples, "
") #> Nested samples: TRUE cat("Nesting structure:", lpq_nested@comparison$nesting_structure, "
") #> Nesting structure: rarefied (143 samples) is nested in original (185 samples)

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: phyloseq objects different samples shared modalities sample_data (e.g., experimental conditions, sites), comparison classified EXPLORATION. type detected automatically based sample_data.
code:
# Simulate by subsetting samples from different parts of the dataset # In real use, these would be different sample groups with shared experimental factors sample_names_fungi <- sample_names(data_fungi) n_samples <- length(sample_names_fungi)  # Split samples into two groups (simulating different sample sets with shared modalities) group1_samples <- sample_names_fungi[1:floor(n_samples / 2)] group2_samples <- sample_names_fungi[(floor(n_samples / 2) + 1):n_samples]  data_group1 <- prune_samples(group1_samples, data_fungi) data_group2 <- prune_samples(group2_samples, data_fungi)  lpq_exploration <- list_phyloseq(list(   group1 = data_group1,   group2 = data_group2 ))  cat("Type:", lpq_exploration@comparison$type_of_comparison, "
") #> Type: EXPLORATION cat("Same samples:", lpq_exploration@comparison$same_samples, "
") #> Same samples: FALSE cat("Common samples:", lpq_exploration@comparison$n_common_samples, "
") #> Common samples: 0

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: phyloseq objects different samples shared modalities, comparison classified SEPARATE_ANALYSIS. Direct comparison may meaningful. Separate analysis phyloseq object recommended.
code: # Example using completely different datasets data("enterotype", package = "phyloseq")  lpq_separate <- list_phyloseq(list(   fungi = data_fungi,   enterotype = enterotype ))  cat("Type:", lpq_separate@comparison$type_of_comparison, "\n") #> Type: SEPARATE_ANALYSIS cat("Same samples:", lpq_separate@comparison$same_samples, "\n") #> Same samples: FALSE

title: The list_phyloseq Class
code:
# Start with a single phyloseq lpq_single <- list_phyloseq(list(fungi = data_fungi)) cat("Initial number of phyloseq objects:", length(lpq_single), "
") #> Initial number of phyloseq objects: 1  # Add another phyloseq object lpq_added <- add_phyloseq(lpq_single, data_fungi_mini, name = "fungi_mini") cat("After adding:", length(lpq_added), "
") #> After adding: 2 cat("Names:", paste(names(lpq_added), collapse = ", "), "
") #> Names: fungi, fungi_mini  # Remove a phyloseq object lpq_removed <- remove_phyloseq(lpq_added, "fungi_mini") cat("After removing:", length(lpq_removed), "
") #> After removing: 1

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: modify phyloseq objects inside list_phyloseq, use update_list_phyloseq() recompute summary table comparison characteristics. can also use function change same_primer_seq_tech same_bioinfo_pipeline parameters:
code: lpq@phyloseq_list[[1]] <- rarefy_even_depth(lpq@phyloseq_list[[1]]) lpq_updated <- update_list_phyloseq(lpq)  # Change the comparison type by modifying parameters lpq_robustness <- update_list_phyloseq(lpq, same_bioinfo_pipeline = FALSE) cat("New type:", lpq_robustness@comparison$type_of_comparison, "\n")

what: Filtering to Common Samples and Taxa
title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: filter_common_lpq() function filters phyloseq object retain samples /taxa common across objects. particularly useful : NESTED_ROBUSTNESS comparisons: filter common samples comparing original vs rarefied comparison need phyloseq objects samples/taxa can also filter common taxa:
code:
# Create a list_phyloseq with nested samples set.seed(123) lpq_to_filter <- list_phyloseq(list(   original = data_fungi,   rarefied = rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi, sample.size = 1000, verbose = FALSE) ))  # Before filtering cat("Before filtering:
") #> Before filtering: cat("  Original samples:", nsamples(lpq_to_filter[["original"]]), "
") #>   Original samples: 185 cat("  Rarefied samples:", nsamples(lpq_to_filter[["rarefied"]]), "
") #>   Rarefied samples: 143 cat("  Common samples:", lpq_to_filter@comparison$n_common_samples, "
") #>   Common samples: 143  # Filter to keep only common samples lpq_filtered <- filter_common_lpq(lpq_to_filter,   filter_samples = TRUE,   filter_taxa = FALSE,   verbose = TRUE )  # After filtering - both have the same samples cat("
After filtering:
") #>  #> After filtering: cat("  Original samples:", nsamples(lpq_filtered[["original"]]), "
") #>   Original samples: 143 cat("  Rarefied samples:", nsamples(lpq_filtered[["rarefied"]]), "
") #>   Rarefied samples: 143 cat("  Same samples now:", lpq_filtered@comparison$same_samples, "
") #>   Same samples now: TRUE # Filter both samples and taxa lpq_filtered_both <- filter_common_lpq(lpq_to_filter,   filter_samples = TRUE,   filter_taxa = TRUE )

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: shared_mod_lpq() function displays sample_data variable modalities shared across phyloseq objects: useful understanding factors common across datasets, especially EXPLORATION type comparisons.
code: shared_mod_lpq(lpq) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   Variable     N_shared Shared_modalities                                        #>   <chr>           <int> <chr>                                                    #> 1 X                 137 A10-005-B_S188_MERGED.fastq.gz, A10-005-H_S189_MERGED.f… #> 2 Sample_names      137 A10-005-B_S188, A10-005-H_S189, A10-005-M_S190, A12-007… #> 3 Tree_name         100 A10-005, A12-007, A15-004, A8-005, AB29-abm-X, AC27-013… #> 4 Height              4 Low, High, Middle, NA                                    #> 5 Diameter           70 52, 28,4, 30,7, 32,8, 33,3, 99, 32, 55,4, 115,5, -, 10,…

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: formattable_lpq() function creates beautiful HTML table showing summary statistics colored bars indicators: complete view including comparison characteristics:
code: # Basic formattable visualization formattable_lpq(lpq)  # Custom columns selection formattable_lpq(lpq, columns = c("name", "n_samples", "n_taxa", "n_sequences"))  # Custom colors for bars formattable_lpq(lpq, bar_colors = list(   n_samples = "steelblue",   n_taxa = "darkgreen" )) # Get both summary and comparison tables result <- formattable_lpq_full(lpq) result$summary result$comparison

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: upset_lpq() function creates UpSet plots Venn diagrams showing shared taxonomic values across phyloseq objects:
code: # Create a list_phyloseq with multiple objects lpq_multi <- list_phyloseq(list(   fungi = data_fungi,   fungi_mini = data_fungi_mini ))  # Venn diagram (automatically chosen for ≤4 sets) upset_lpq(lpq_multi, tax_rank = "Family") # Force UpSet plot (better for more than 4 sets or complex intersections) data("enterotype", package = "phyloseq") lpq_many <- list_phyloseq(list(   fungi = data_fungi,   fungi_mini = data_fungi_mini,   fungi_rarefied = rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi, sample.size = 1000, verbose = FALSE),   enterotype = enterotype ))  upset_lpq(lpq_many, tax_rank = "Genus", plot_type = "upset")

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: list_phyloseq class supports standard R subsetting operations:
code: # Get the number of phyloseq objects length(lpq) #> [1] 2  # Get names names(lpq) #> [1] "fungi"      "fungi_mini"  # Extract a single phyloseq object pq1 <- lpq[["fungi"]] cat("Extracted phyloseq has", ntaxa(pq1), "taxa\n") #> Extracted phyloseq has 1420 taxa  # Subset to create a new list_phyloseq with fewer objects lpq_subset <- lpq[1] # Returns a new list_phyloseq with only the first object

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: ’s complete workflow comparing effect rarefaction data:
code: # Step 1: Create original and rarefied versions set.seed(42) original <- data_fungi rarefied <- rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi, sample.size = 1000, verbose = FALSE)  # Step 2: Create list_phyloseq lpq_rarefaction <- list_phyloseq(list(   original = original,   rarefied = rarefied ))  # Step 3: Check the comparison type print(lpq_rarefaction) #> list_phyloseq object with 2 phyloseq objects #>  #> --- Summary --- #> # A tibble: 2 × 17 #>   name   n_samples n_taxa n_sequences n_occurence mean_seq_length min_seq_length #>   <chr>      <int>  <int>       <dbl>       <int>           <dbl>          <int> #> 1 origi…       185   1420     1839124       12499            318.            251 #> 2 raref…       143   1397      143000        5900            318.            251 #> # ℹ 10 more variables: mean_seq_per_sample <dbl>, sd_seq_per_sample <dbl>, #> #   min_seq_per_sample <dbl>, max_seq_per_sample <dbl>, #> #   mean_seq_per_taxon <dbl>, sd_seq_per_taxon <dbl>, has_sam_data <lgl>, #> #   has_tax_table <lgl>, has_refseq <lgl>, has_phy_tree <lgl> #>  #> --- Comparison characteristics --- #> Type of comparison: NESTED_ROBUSTNESS  #> Nested samples detected (one phyloseq derived from another). #>   Nesting: rarefied (143 samples) is nested in original (185 samples) #>   Useful to test robustness to data processing (e.g., rarefaction). #>   Comparisons should focus on the common (nested) samples.  #>  #> Same primer/seq tech: TRUE  #> Same bioinfo pipeline: TRUE  #> Same sample_data structure: TRUE  #> Same samples: FALSE  #> Nested samples: TRUE  #> Same taxa: FALSE  #> Common samples: 143  #> Common taxa: 1397  #>  #> --- Reference sequence comparison --- #> original_vs_rarefied: 1397 shared seqs, 23 unique in original, 0 unique in rarefied  # Step 4: Filter to common samples for fair comparison lpq_common <- filter_common_lpq(lpq_rarefaction,   filter_samples = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE )  # Step 5: Visualize shared taxa at different ranks upset_lpq(lpq_common, tax_rank = "Family") # Step 6: View the formatted summary formattable_lpq(lpq_common)

title: The list_phyloseq Class
text: list_phyloseq class provides powerful framework comparing multiple phyloseq objects: Precise comparison type determined same_primer_seq_tech same_bioinfo_pipeline parameters combined automatic detection sample overlap Summary statistics computed phyloseq object Utility functions adding, removing, filtering, updating Visualization functions exploring differences (formattable tables, UpSet/Venn plots) Key points: Set same_bioinfo_pipeline = FALSE ROBUSTNESS comparisons (different pipelines) Set same_primer_seq_tech = FALSE REPLICABILITY comparisons (different primers/technologies) NESTED_ROBUSTNESS, EXPLORATION, SEPARATE_ANALYSIS detected automatically NESTED_ROBUSTNESS comparisons, use filter_common_lpq() focus common samples upset_lpq() function excellent visualizing shared taxonomic composition Use update_list_phyloseq() change parameters recompute modifications

what: An extension to {MiscMetabar} and {phyloseq} package to compare phyloseq object
title: An extension to {MiscMetabar} and {phyloseq} package to compare phyloseq object
text: comparpq extension {MiscMetabar} {phyloseq} packages provides tools comparing phyloseq objects. package focuses taxonomic comparison, accuracy metrics computation, interactive visualizations microbiome data analysis.

title: An extension to {MiscMetabar} and {phyloseq} package to compare phyloseq object
text: can install development version comparpq GitHub :
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("adrientaudiere/comparpq")

title: An extension to {MiscMetabar} and {phyloseq} package to compare phyloseq object
text: list_phyloseq: S7 class storing comparing multiple phyloseq objects, automatic detection comparison type (REPRODUCIBILITY, ROBUSTNESS, NESTED_ROBUSTNESS, REPLICABILITY, EXPLORATION, SEPARATE_ANALYSIS) compare_refseq(): Compare reference sequences two phyloseq objects, identifying shared/unique ASVs name DNA sequence content filter_common_lpq(): Filter phyloseq objects keep shared samples /taxa apply_to_lpq(): Apply function phyloseq objects list_phyloseq

title: An extension to {MiscMetabar} and {phyloseq} package to compare phyloseq object
text: adonis_lpq(): PERMANOVA analysis across multiple phyloseq objects estim_diff_pq() & estim_diff_lpq(): Estimation statistics categorical comparisons using bootstrap confidence intervals (via dabestr) estim_cor_pq() & estim_cor_lpq(): Estimation statistics continuous correlations bootstrap confidence intervals ancombc_lpq(), aldex_lpq(), multipatt_lpq(), maaslin3_pq(): Differential abundance analysis methods

title: An extension to {MiscMetabar} and {phyloseq} package to compare phyloseq object
text: tc_metrics_mock(): Compute accuracy metrics comparing taxonomic assignations mock communities tc_metrics_mock_vec(): Vectorized version efficient metric computation tc_points_matrix(): Create matrices taxonomic comparison visualization

title: An extension to {MiscMetabar} and {phyloseq} package to compare phyloseq object
text: bubbles_pq(): Interactive bubble plots using Observable HQ phyloseq objects tc_bar() & tc_circle(): Bar charts circular plots taxonomic comparisons tc_sankey(): Sankey diagrams taxonomic rank relationships tc_linked_trees(): Side--side linked taxonomy trees comparing two assignments tc_congruence_metrics(): Congruence metrics two taxonomic assignments rainplot_taxo_na(): Rain plots visualizing NA patterns taxonomic data formattable_lpq() & formattable_lpq_full(): Formatted summary tables color bars list_phyloseq upset_lpq(): UpSet plots visualizing sample/taxa overlap across phyloseq objects gg_aldex_plot() & gg_maaslin3_plot(): ggplot2-based visualizations differential abundance results

title: An extension to {MiscMetabar} and {phyloseq} package to compare phyloseq object
text: Mock community preparation: add_external_seq_pq(), add_shuffle_seq_pq(), multiply_counts_pq(), permute_da_pq(), midasim_pq() creating manipulating fake taxa benchmark taxonomic assignment differential abundance methods Taxonomic table utilities: rename_ranks_pq(), select_ranks_pq(), resolve_taxo_conflict(), taxtab_replace_pattern_by_NA() Taxonomy tree: taxo2tree() build phylogenetic tree taxonomy table

title: An extension to {MiscMetabar} and {phyloseq} package to compare phyloseq object
code: library(comparpq)  # Load example data data("Glom_otu")  # Create an interactive bubble plot bubbles_pq(Glom_otu,            rank_color = "Family",            min_nb_seq = 1000)  # For taxonomic comparison analysis with mock communities: # 1. First prepare your data with fake taxa physeq_with_fake <- add_shuffle_seq_pq(Glom_otu, nb_seq_fake = 50)  # 2. Perform taxonomic assignment (using your preferred method) # 3. Compare results against known taxonomy # tc_metrics_mock(physeq_with_fake, ranks_df, true_values_df)

title: PERMANOVA analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — adonis_lpq
text: Performs PERMANOVA analysis using MiscMetabar::adonis_pq() phyloseq object list_phyloseq returns summary table results.

title: PERMANOVA analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — adonis_lpq
code: adonis_lpq(   x,   formula,   dist_method = "bray",   na_remove = FALSE,   correction_for_sample_size = FALSE,   rarefy_nb_seqs = FALSE,   by = "margin",   verbose = TRUE,   ... )

title: PERMANOVA analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — adonis_lpq
text: x (required) list_phyloseq object. formula (character, required) right part formula vegan::adonis2(). Variables must present sample_data slot phyloseq objects. formula contain variables shared modalities. dist_method (character, default "bray") distance method use. See phyloseq::distance() available methods. na_remove (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, samples NA values formula variables removed analysis. correction_for_sample_size (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, adds library size formula following Weiss et al. 2017 recommendations. rarefy_nb_seqs (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, rarefy sample computing distances. (character, default "margin") argument passed vegan::adonis2(). Options "terms", "margin", NULL. verbose (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, print progress messages. ... Additional arguments passed MiscMetabar::adonis_pq().

title: PERMANOVA analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — adonis_lpq
text: tibble one row per phyloseq object following columns: name Name phyloseq object term term(s) formula (one row per term multiple) Df Degrees freedom SumOfSqs Sum squares R2 R-squared value F F statistic Pr(>F) P-value partial_R2 Partial R-squared (available)

title: PERMANOVA analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — adonis_lpq
text: function requires list_phyloseq type SEPARATE_ANALYSIS, formula must contain variables common across phyloseq objects. function wrapper around MiscMetabar::adonis_pq(), wraps vegan::adonis2().

text: x (required) list_phyloseq object. bifactor (character, required) name dichotomous column sample_data use grouping factor. Must present phyloseq objects. modalities (character vector, default NULL) Two modalities bifactor compare. NULL, uses two levels present data. gamma (numeric, default 0.5) gamma parameter ALDEx2. verbose (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, print progress messages. ... Additional arguments passed MiscMetabar::aldex_pq().

title: ALDEx2 analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — aldex_lpq
text: tibble combined ALDEx2 results phyloseq objects. row corresponds one taxon one phyloseq object, columns ALDEx2 output plus taxon (rownames) name (identifying source phyloseq object).

title: ALDEx2 analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — aldex_lpq
text: function requires list_phyloseq type SEPARATE_ANALYSIS, bifactor must common across phyloseq objects. common taxa exist across phyloseq objects, taxa names suffixed phyloseq object name make distinguishable.

title: ALDEx2 analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — aldex_lpq
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lpq <- list_phyloseq(   list(     fungi = data_fungi,     fungi_clust = postcluster_pq(data_fungi)   ),   same_bioinfo_pipeline = FALSE )  results <- aldex_lpq(lpq,   bifactor = "Height",   modalities = c("Low", "High") ) results  ALDEx2::aldex.plot(filter(results, name == "fungi"), type = "volcano") ALDEx2::aldex.plot(filter(results, name == "fungi_clust"), type = "volcano")  ggplot(results, aes(y = taxon, x = effect, col = wi.eBH)) +   geom_point() } # }

what: ANCOM-BC analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — ancombc_lpq
title: ANCOM-BC analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — ancombc_lpq
text: Performs ANCOM-BC differential abundance analysis using MiscMetabar::ancombc_pq() phyloseq object list_phyloseq returns combined result table.

title: ANCOM-BC analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — ancombc_lpq
code: ancombc_lpq(x, fact, levels_fact = NULL, tax_level = NULL, verbose = TRUE, ...)

title: ANCOM-BC analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — ancombc_lpq
text: x (required) list_phyloseq object. fact (character, required) name column sample_data use grouping factor. Must present phyloseq objects. levels_fact (character vector, default NULL) Levels factor include. NULL, levels used. tax_level (character, default "Class") Taxonomic level agglomeration analysis. verbose (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, print progress messages. ... Additional arguments passed MiscMetabar::ancombc_pq().

title: ANCOM-BC analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — ancombc_lpq
text: tibble combined ANCOM-BC results phyloseq objects. Contains $res data frame ANCOM-BC run, additional name column identifying source phyloseq object.

title: ANCOM-BC analysis on each phyloseq object in a list_phyloseq — ancombc_lpq
text: function requires list_phyloseq type SEPARATE_ANALYSIS, factor must common across phyloseq objects. common taxa exist across phyloseq objects, taxa names suffixed phyloseq object name make distinguishable.

what: Apply a function to all phyloseq objects in a list_phyloseq — apply_to_lpq
title: Apply a function to all phyloseq objects in a list_phyloseq — apply_to_lpq
text: Applies function phyloseq object list_phyloseq returns new list_phyloseq transformed objects. useful applying cleaning, filtering, transformation functions uniformly across phyloseq objects.

title: Apply a function to all phyloseq objects in a list_phyloseq — apply_to_lpq
code: apply_to_lpq(x, .f, ..., verbose = TRUE)

title: Apply a function to all phyloseq objects in a list_phyloseq — apply_to_lpq
text: x (required) list_phyloseq object. .f (function, required) function apply phyloseq object. function must take phyloseq object first argument return phyloseq object. ... Additional arguments passed .f. verbose (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, print information transformation applied phyloseq object.

title: Apply a function to all phyloseq objects in a list_phyloseq — apply_to_lpq
text: new list_phyloseq object transformed phyloseq objects. comparison parameters (same_primer_seq_tech, same_bioinfo_pipeline) preserved original object.

title: Apply a function to all phyloseq objects in a list_phyloseq — apply_to_lpq
text: Common functions apply include: MiscMetabar::clean_pq(): Remove empty samples taxa phyloseq::taxa_as_rows(): Ensure taxa rows otu_table phyloseq::rarefy_even_depth(): Rarefy even depth phyloseq::transform_sample_counts(): Transform counts (e.g., relative abundance) phyloseq::subset_taxa(): Filter taxa based criteria phyloseq::subset_samples(): Filter samples based criteria

title: Apply a function to all phyloseq objects in a list_phyloseq — apply_to_lpq
text: Adrien Taudière

what: Bubble plot of phyloseq object with observablehq   — bubbles_pq
title: Bubble plot of phyloseq object with observablehq   — bubbles_pq
text: Creates interactive bubble plot visualization taxa abundances phyloseq object using Observable notebook.

title: Bubble plot of phyloseq object with observablehq   — bubbles_pq
code: bubbles_pq(   physeq,   rank_label = "Taxa",   rank_color = "Family",   categorical_scheme = "d3.schemeCategory10",   label_color = "grey10",   value_color = "grey20",   label_size = 8,   log1ptransform = FALSE,   min_nb_seq = 0,   randomize = FALSE,   seed = 32,   width = 600,   include = c("TitleCell", "key", "chart"),   notebook = "https://observablehq.com/d/d755af3197af2320",   return_dataframe = FALSE,   title = "",   title_size = "22px" )

title: Bubble plot of phyloseq object with observablehq   — bubbles_pq
text: physeq (required) phyloseq-class object obtained using phyloseq package. rank_label (character, default "Taxa") name column @tax_table slot label points. set "Taxa", taxa name used. rank_color (character, default "Family") name column @tax_table slot color points. categorical_scheme (character, default "d3.schemeCategory10") color scheme d3js. label_color (character, default "grey10") Color label text. value_color (character, default "grey20") Color value text. label_size (integer, default 8) Font size label. log1ptransform (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, number sequences log1p transformed. min_nb_seq (integer, default 0) Minimum number sequences filter taxa low abundance. randomize (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, shuffles order taxa. seed (integer, default 32) Seed randomization. width (integer, default 600) notebook width visualization pixels. include (character vector, default c("TitleCell", "key", "chart")) can modify include remove title /legend, example using include = c("chart"). notebook (character, default "https://observablehq.com/d/d755af3197af2320") can change notebook url know . return_dataframe (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, plot returned, resulting dataframe plot returned. title (character, default "") Title plot. title_size (character, default "22px") Font size title.

what: Compare refseq slots between two phyloseq objects — compare_refseq
title: Compare refseq slots between two phyloseq objects — compare_refseq
text: Performs detailed comparison reference sequences (refseq slot) two phyloseq objects. Identifies shared unique ASVs/OTUs taxa name actual DNA sequence content. useful detect subtle differences two phyloseq objects may share samples differ ASV/OTU composition, e.g. different bioinformatics pipelines filtering steps. unique (non-shared) sequences exist, mean nearest-neighbor k-mer distance unique sequence set computed. gives sense different unmatched sequences closest counterpart object.

title: Compare refseq slots between two phyloseq objects — compare_refseq
code: compare_refseq(   physeq1,   physeq2 = NULL,   name1 = NULL,   name2 = NULL,   k = 5,   max_seqs = 500,   seed = NULL,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Compare refseq slots between two phyloseq objects — compare_refseq
text: physeq1 (phyloseq list_phyloseq, required) First phyloseq object, must refseq slot. Alternatively, list_phyloseq object; case first two phyloseq objects used (names) physeq2 ignored. physeq2 (phyloseq, default NULL) Second phyloseq object. Must refseq slot. Ignored physeq1 list_phyloseq. name1 (character, default NULL) Label first phyloseq object output. NULL, inferred list_phyloseq names defaults "physeq1". name2 (character, default NULL) Label second phyloseq object output. NULL, inferred list_phyloseq names defaults "physeq2". k (integer, default 5) k-mer size nearest-neighbor distance computation unique sequences. max_seqs (integer, default 500) Maximum number unique sequences per object use distance computation. , random sample drawn. Set Inf use (may slow). seed (integer, default NULL) Random seed reproducible sampling max_seqs exceeded. verbose (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, print summary comparison.

title: Compare refseq slots between two phyloseq objects — compare_refseq
text: list class "compare_refseq" components: n_taxa Named integer vector number taxa object. shared_names Character vector taxa names present objects. unique_names_1 Taxa names physeq1. unique_names_2 Taxa names physeq2. shared_seqs Character vector unique DNA sequences found objects (regardless taxa name). unique_seqs_1 DNA sequences physeq1. unique_seqs_2 DNA sequences physeq2. same_name_diff_seq data frame taxa name different sequences across objects. Columns: taxa_name, seq_physeq1, seq_physeq2. same_seq_diff_name data frame sequences shared objects different names. Columns: sequence, name_physeq1, name_physeq2. mean_nn_kmer_dist_1 Mean nearest-neighbor k-mer distance sequences unique physeq1 closest sequence physeq2. NA unique sequences. mean_nn_kmer_dist_2 Mean nearest-neighbor k-mer distance sequences unique physeq2 closest sequence physeq1. NA unique sequences.

title: Compare refseq slots between two phyloseq objects — compare_refseq
text: Adrien Taudiere

what: Diversity indices per sample, optionally grouped by a modality — div_pq
title: Diversity indices per sample, optionally grouped by a modality — div_pq
text: Computes alpha-diversity indices (via vegan::diversity()) /Hill numbers / Rényi entropy (via vegan::renyi()) sample phyloseq object. modality supplied computation done separately level grouping variable level label appended extra column. NA values modality column kept distinct group samples missing metadata never silently dropped. modality = NULL samples analysed together grouping column added result.

title: Diversity indices per sample, optionally grouped by a modality — div_pq
code: div_pq(   physeq,   modality = NULL,   indices = "shannon",   scales = NULL,   hill = TRUE,   aggregate = FALSE,   funs = list(mean = mean, sd = sd) )

title: Diversity indices per sample, optionally grouped by a modality — div_pq
text: physeq (phyloseq, required) phyloseq object. modality (character NULL, default NULL) Name column sample_data(physeq) used split samples groups. NA values treated separate group. NULL, indices computed whole dataset without grouping. indices (character, default "shannon") One index names accepted vegan::diversity() (e.g. "shannon", "simpson", "invsimpson"). Set NULL skip classical diversity indices. scales (numeric NULL, default NULL) Scale values passed vegan::renyi(). NULL, Hill / Rényi computation skipped. hill (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, return Hill numbers; FALSE, return Rényi entropy. Passed vegan::renyi(). relevant scales NULL. aggregate (logical, default FALSE) TRUE modality NULL, summarise per-group results using funs. funs (named list, default list(mean = mean, sd = sd)) Summary functions applied aggregate = TRUE. Passed dplyr::across().

title: Diversity indices per sample, optionally grouped by a modality — div_pq
text: data frame one row per sample. Columns correspond requested indices /Hill / Rényi scales. modality supplied, additional column named modality identifies group (possibly NA). aggregate = TRUE rows collapsed one per group summary statistics.

title: Diversity indices per sample, optionally grouped by a modality — div_pq
text: Adrien Taudière

title: Diversity indices per sample, optionally grouped by a modality — div_pq

what: Estimation statistics for numeric correlation on a list_phyloseq — estim_cor_lpq
title: Estimation statistics for numeric correlation on a list_phyloseq — estim_cor_lpq
text: Applies estim_cor_pq() phyloseq object list_phyloseq combines results.

title: Estimation statistics for numeric correlation on a list_phyloseq — estim_cor_lpq
code: estim_cor_lpq(x, variable, ..., verbose = TRUE)

title: Estimation statistics for numeric correlation on a list_phyloseq — estim_cor_lpq
text: x (required) list_phyloseq object. variable (character, required) name numeric column sample_data. Must present phyloseq objects. ... Additional arguments passed estim_cor_pq(). verbose (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, print progress messages.

title: Estimation statistics for numeric correlation on a list_phyloseq — estim_cor_lpq
text: list class "estim_cor_lpq_result" components: results named list estim_cor_pq_result objects (one per phyloseq) correlations tibble combining correlations additional name column regressions tibble combining regressions additional name column

title: Estimation statistics for numeric correlation on a list_phyloseq — estim_cor_lpq
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ lpq <- list_phyloseq(   list(     fungi = data_fungi,     fungi_clust = postcluster_pq(data_fungi)   ),   same_bioinfo_pipeline = FALSE )  # Assuming a numeric variable exists in sample_data results <- estim_cor_lpq(lpq, variable = "lib_size") results$correlations } # }

what: Estimation statistics for numeric variable correlation on a phyloseq object — estim_cor_pq
title: Estimation statistics for numeric variable correlation on a phyloseq object — estim_cor_pq
text: Computes diversity metrics (Hill numbers default) per sample assesses relationship numeric variable using bootstrap confidence intervals correlation coefficients regression slopes.

title: Estimation statistics for numeric variable correlation on a phyloseq object — estim_cor_pq
code: estim_cor_pq(   physeq,   variable,   hill_scales = c(0, 1, 2),   custom_fn = NULL,   method = "pearson",   resamples = 5000,   ci = 95,   na_remove = TRUE )

title: Estimation statistics for numeric variable correlation on a phyloseq object — estim_cor_pq
text: physeq (phyloseq, required) phyloseq object. variable (character, required) name numeric column sample_data. hill_scales (numeric vector, default c(0, 1, 2)) q values Hill number computation. custom_fn (function, default NULL) optional custom diversity function (see estim_diff_pq() details). method (character, default "pearson") Correlation method. One "pearson", "spearman", "kendall". resamples (integer, default 5000) Number bootstrap resamples. ci (numeric, default 95) Confidence interval level (0-100). na_remove (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, samples NA variable removed.

title: Estimation statistics for numeric variable correlation on a phyloseq object — estim_cor_pq
text: list class "estim_cor_pq_result" components: data diversity data.frame used analysis correlations tibble columns: metric, estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper, method, pvalue regressions tibble columns: metric, intercept, slope, slope_ci_lower, slope_ci_upper plots named list ggplot2 scatter plots regression line bootstrap CI ribbon (one per metric)

title: Estimation statistics for numeric variable correlation on a phyloseq object — estim_cor_pq
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(phyloseq) data("data_fungi", package = "MiscMetabar")  # Add a numeric variable for demonstration sam <- sample_data(data_fungi) sam$lib_size <- sample_sums(data_fungi) sample_data(data_fungi) <- sam  res <- estim_cor_pq(data_fungi, variable = "lib_size") res res$plots$Hill_0 res$correlations } # }

what: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a list_phyloseq — estim_diff_lpq
title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a list_phyloseq — estim_diff_lpq
text: Applies estim_diff_pq() phyloseq object list_phyloseq combines results. allows comparing estimation statistics across different bioinformatic pipelines parameter settings.

title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a list_phyloseq — estim_diff_lpq
code: estim_diff_lpq(x, fact, ..., verbose = TRUE)

title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a list_phyloseq — estim_diff_lpq
text: x (required) list_phyloseq object. fact (character, required) name categorical column sample_data use grouping factor. Must present phyloseq objects. ... Additional arguments passed estim_diff_pq(). verbose (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, print progress messages.

title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a list_phyloseq — estim_diff_lpq
text: list class "estim_diff_lpq_result" components: results named list estim_diff_pq_result objects (one per phyloseq) summary tibble combining summaries additional name column identifying source phyloseq

title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a list_phyloseq — estim_diff_lpq

what: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a phyloseq object — estim_diff_pq
title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a phyloseq object — estim_diff_pq
text: Computes diversity metrics (Hill numbers default) per sample compares across groups defined categorical variable using estimation statistics (effect sizes + bootstrap confidence intervals) via dabestr package. approach replaces traditional p-value-based hypothesis testing Gardner-Altman Cumming estimation plots, following estimation statistics framework (Ho et al. 2019).

title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a phyloseq object — estim_diff_pq
code: estim_diff_pq(   physeq,   fact,   hill_scales = c(0, 1, 2),   custom_fn = NULL,   effect_type = "cohens_d",   idx = NULL,   ci = 95,   resamples = 5000,   na_remove = TRUE,   ... )

title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a phyloseq object — estim_diff_pq
text: physeq (phyloseq, required) phyloseq object. fact (character, required) name categorical column sample_data use grouping factor. hill_scales (numeric vector, default c(0, 1, 2)) q values Hill number computation: 0 = richness, 1 = Shannon exponential, 2 = inverse Simpson. custom_fn (function, default NULL) optional custom diversity function. Must take phyloseq object return named numeric vector (names = sample names) data.frame one row per sample. provided, hill_scales ignored. effect_type (character, default "mean_diff") type effect size compute. One : "mean_diff", "median_diff", "cohens_d", "hedges_g", "cliffs_delta". idx (list character vector, default NULL) group ordering comparisons. NULL, uses factor levels first level control. 2 groups: c("Control", "Treatment"). 3+ groups: list(c("Ctrl", "T1", "T2")). ci (numeric, default 95) Confidence interval level (0-100). resamples (integer, default 5000) Number bootstrap resamples. na_remove (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, samples NA fact removed analysis. ... Additional arguments passed dabestr plotting functions.

title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a phyloseq object — estim_diff_pq
text: list class "estim_diff_pq_result" components: data diversity data.frame used analysis dabest_objects named list dabestr objects (one per metric) plots named list dabestr plots (one per metric) summary tibble summarizing effect sizes CIs columns: metric, comparison, effect_size, ci_lower, ci_upper, pvalue_permtest, pvalue_welch, pvalue_mann_whitney effect_type effect size type used

title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a phyloseq object — estim_diff_pq
text: function uses dabestr package produce estimation plots. two groups, Gardner-Altman plots produced (float_contrast = TRUE). three groups, Cumming plots used (float_contrast = FALSE).

title: Estimation statistics for categorical comparisons on a phyloseq object — estim_diff_pq
text: Ho, J., Tumkaya, T., Aryal, S., Choi, H., & Claridge-Chang, . (2019). Moving beyond P values: data analysis estimation graphics. Nature Methods, 16(7), 565-566.

what: Filter phyloseq objects to keep only shared samples and/or taxa — filter_common_lpq
title: Filter phyloseq objects to keep only shared samples and/or taxa — filter_common_lpq
text: Filters phyloseq object list_phyloseq retain samples /taxa common across objects. useful making direct comparisons common basis.

title: Filter phyloseq objects to keep only shared samples and/or taxa — filter_common_lpq
code: filter_common_lpq(   x,   filter_samples = TRUE,   filter_taxa = FALSE,   clean = TRUE,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Filter phyloseq objects to keep only shared samples and/or taxa — filter_common_lpq
text: x (required) list_phyloseq object. filter_samples (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, filter keep samples present phyloseq objects. filter_taxa (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, filter keep taxa present phyloseq objects. clean (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, apply MiscMetabar::clean_pq() filtering remove empty samples/taxa. verbose (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, print information filtering process.

title: Filter phyloseq objects to keep only shared samples and/or taxa — filter_common_lpq
text: new list_phyloseq object filtered phyloseq objects

title: Filter phyloseq objects to keep only shared samples and/or taxa — filter_common_lpq
text: function particularly useful : NESTED_ROBUSTNESS comparisons: filter common samples comparing original vs rarefied data EXPLORATION comparisons: filter common samples/taxa comparing different sample groups comparison need ensure phyloseq objects contain samples taxa

title: Filter phyloseq objects to keep only shared samples and/or taxa — filter_common_lpq
code: lpq <- list_phyloseq(list(   original = data_fungi,   mini = data_fungi_mini,   rarefied = rarefy_even_depth(data_fungi) )) #> You set `rngseed` to FALSE. Make sure you've set & recorded #>  the random seed of your session for reproducibility. #> See `?set.seed` #> ... #> 1007OTUs were removed because they are no longer  #> present in any sample after random subsampling #> ... #> ℹ Building summary table for 3 phyloseq objects... #> ℹ Computing comparison characteristics... #> ℹ Checking sample and taxa overlap... #> ℹ Detected comparison type: NESTED_ROBUSTNESS #> ℹ 137 common samples, 42 common taxa #> ✔ list_phyloseq created (NESTED_ROBUSTNESS)  # Filter to keep only common samples (useful for nested comparisons) lpq_filtered <- filter_common_lpq(lpq, filter_samples = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) #> Cleaning suppress 10 taxa and 0 samples. #> Cleaning suppress 61 taxa and 0 samples.  # Filter to keep only common taxa lpq_filtered <- filter_common_lpq(lpq, filter_samples = FALSE, filter_taxa = TRUE) #> Filtering to 42 common taxa. #> Cleaning suppress 0 taxa and 48 samples. #>   original: 185 -> 137 samples, 1420 -> 42 taxa #>   mini: 137 -> 137 samples, 45 -> 42 taxa #> Cleaning suppress 0 taxa and 115 samples. #>   rarefied: 185 -> 70 samples, 413 -> 42 taxa #> ℹ Building summary table for 3 phyloseq objects... #> ℹ Computing comparison characteristics... #> ℹ Checking sample and taxa overlap... #> ℹ Detected comparison type: NESTED_ROBUSTNESS #> ℹ 70 common samples, 42 common taxa #> ✔ list_phyloseq created (NESTED_ROBUSTNESS)  # Filter both samples and taxa lpq_filtered <- filter_common_lpq(lpq, filter_samples = TRUE, filter_taxa = TRUE) #> Filtering to 137 common samples. #> Filtering to 42 common taxa. #>   original: 185 -> 137 samples, 1420 -> 42 taxa #>   mini: 137 -> 137 samples, 45 -> 42 taxa #> Cleaning suppress 0 taxa and 67 samples. #>   rarefied: 185 -> 70 samples, 413 -> 42 taxa #> ℹ Building summary table for 3 phyloseq objects... #> ℹ Computing comparison characteristics... #> ℹ Checking sample and taxa overlap... #> ℹ Detected comparison type: NESTED_ROBUSTNESS #> ℹ 70 common samples, 42 common taxa #> ✔ list_phyloseq created (NESTED_ROBUSTNESS)

what: Formattable visualization for list_phyloseq summary — formattable_lpq
title: Formattable visualization for list_phyloseq summary — formattable_lpq

title: Formattable visualization for list_phyloseq summary — formattable_lpq
code: formattable_lpq(   x,   columns = c("name", "n_samples", "n_taxa", "n_sequences", "mean_seq_per_sample",     "mean_seq_per_taxon", "has_sam_data", "has_tax_table", "has_refseq", "has_phy_tree"),   bar_colors = NULL,   round_digits = 1,   void_style = FALSE,   log10_transform = TRUE,   ... )

title: Formattable visualization for list_phyloseq summary — formattable_lpq
text: x (required) list_phyloseq object. columns (character vector, default selection key columns) Character vector column names display. NULL, displays curated selection columns. bar_colors (named list, default NULL) Named list colors numeric columns bars. Names match column names. Default colors provided common columns. round_digits (integer, default 1) Number decimal places rounding numeric columns. void_style (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, returns formattable without custom styling. log10_transform (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, applies log10 transformation numeric columns range greater 1000. ... Additional arguments passed formattable::formattable().

what: ggplot2 version of ALDEx2 diagnostic plots — gg_aldex_plot
title: ggplot2 version of ALDEx2 diagnostic plots — gg_aldex_plot
text: Creates ggplot2 versions four ALDEx2 diagnostic plot types: MW (Bland-Altman style), MA, volcano, volcano.var. Supports faceting name column, making suitable output aldex_lpq().

title: ggplot2 version of ALDEx2 diagnostic plots — gg_aldex_plot
code: gg_aldex_plot(   x,   type = c("MW", "MA", "volcano", "volcano.var"),   test = c("welch", "wilcox", "effect", "both"),   cutoff.pval = 0.05,   cutoff.effect = 1,   rare = 0,   all.col = "grey30",   called.col = "red",   rare.col = "black",   point.size = 1.5,   point.alpha = 0.4 )

title: ggplot2 version of ALDEx2 diagnostic plots — gg_aldex_plot
text: x (data.frame tibble, required) ALDEx2 results, typically ALDEx2::aldex() aldex_lpq(). Must contain columns diff.btw, diff.win, rab., effect. test "welch", column .eBH required; "wilcox", column wi.eBH required. type (character, default "MW") Plot type. One "MW" (dispersion vs difference), "MA" (abundance vs difference), "volcano" (difference vs -log10 q-value), "volcano.var" (dispersion vs -log10 q-value). test (character, default "welch") Statistical test used significance calling. One "welch", "wilcox", "effect", "" (effect + welch). cutoff.pval (numeric, default 0.05) q-value threshold significance. cutoff.effect (numeric, default 1) Effect size threshold (must >= 0.5 test "effect" ""). rare (numeric, default 0) Abundance threshold taxa marked rare (used MW MA plots). .col (character, default "grey30") Color non-significant points. called.col (character, default "red") Color significant points. rare.col (character, default "black") Color rare taxa points. point.size (numeric, default 1.5) Size points. point.alpha (numeric, default 0.4) Alpha transparency non-significant points.

title: ggplot2 version of ALDEx2 diagnostic plots — gg_aldex_plot
text: ggplot2 object. x contains name column (e.g., aldex_lpq()), plot faceted name.

title: ggplot2 version of ALDEx2 diagnostic plots — gg_aldex_plot
text: function reimplements ALDEx2::aldex.plot() using ggplot2, providing customizable composable output. four plot types correspond original ALDEx2 types: MW Bland-Altman style: within-condition dispersion (x) vs -condition difference (y), +/-1 effect size lines. MA MA plot: median log2 relative abundance (x) vs median log2 difference (y). volcano Volcano plot: median log2 difference (x) vs -log10 q-value (y). volcano.var Variance volcano: median log2 dispersion (x) vs -log10 q-value (y).

what: Circle-packed bubble plot of a phyloseq object using ggplot2 — gg_bubbles_pq
title: Circle-packed bubble plot of a phyloseq object using ggplot2 — gg_bubbles_pq
text: Creates static circle-packed bubble plot taxa abundances phyloseq object using ggplot2. Circles can packed circular layout (tight, default) square layout. Optionally facets plot sample data variable, producing one bubble chart per level. list_phyloseq passed physeq, first merged single phyloseq object using merge_lpq() (original phyloseq becomes one sample) plot automatically faceted source_name. diff_contour = TRUE together facet_by (list_phyloseq input), pairwise comparisons facet levels shown side side using patchwork. pair (vs B), taxa unique highlighted 's color taxa unique B B's color. Shared taxa receive transparent contour. makes easy spot taxa exclusive group every pairwise comparison.

title: Circle-packed bubble plot of a phyloseq object using ggplot2 — gg_bubbles_pq
code: gg_bubbles_pq(   physeq,   rank_label = "Taxa",   rank_color = "Family",   rank_contour = NULL,   layout = c("circle", "square"),   facet_by = NULL,   log1ptransform = FALSE,   min_nb_seq = 0,   label_size = 2,   label_color = "grey10",   show_labels = TRUE,   border_color = "white",   border_width = 0.5,   alpha = 0.8,   npoints = 50,   ncol_facet = NULL,   return_dataframe = FALSE,   diff_contour = FALSE,   diff_contour_colors = c("#E41A1C", "#377EB8", "#4DAF4A", "#984EA3"),   diff_border_width = 1.5,   show_title = TRUE,   match_by = c("refseq", "names") )

title: Circle-packed bubble plot of a phyloseq object using ggplot2 — gg_bubbles_pq
text: physeq (required) phyloseq-class object obtained using phyloseq package. rank_label (character, default "Taxa") name column @tax_table slot label circles. set "Taxa", taxa names used. rank_color (character, default "Family") name column @tax_table slot color circles. rank_contour (character, default NULL) name column @tax_table slot color circle borders (contours). NULL, fixed border_color used borders. Ignored diff_contour = TRUE. layout (character, default "circle") packing layout. "circle" produces tight circular packing. "square" constrains circles inside square boundary, large circles placed centrally. facet_by (character, default NULL) column name @sam_data facet plot. set, one bubble chart produced per level variable, taxa abundances computed within level. physeq list_phyloseq, automatically set "source_name". log1ptransform (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, number sequences log1p transformed computing circle sizes. min_nb_seq (integer, default 0) Minimum number sequences filter taxa low abundance. label_size (numeric, default 2) Font size labels inside circles. label_color (character, default "grey10") Color label text. show_labels (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, labels displayed inside circles. circles large enough fit text labeled. border_color (character, default "white") Color circle borders. border_width (numeric, default 0.5) Width circle borders. alpha (numeric, default 0.8) Transparency circle fill. npoints (integer, default 50) Number vertices used approximate circle polygon. Higher values produce smoother circles. ncol_facet (integer, default NULL) Number columns facet layout. Passed ggplot2::facet_wrap(). return_dataframe (logical, default FALSE) TRUE, plot returned, resulting dataframe plot returned. Ignored diff_contour = TRUE. diff_contour (logical, default FALSE) TRUE facet_by set (physeq list_phyloseq), produces pairwise comparison panels pairs facet levels using patchwork. pair, taxa unique side highlighted distinct contour color diff_contour_colors. Shared taxa get transparent contour. TRUE, rank_contour ignored. diff_contour_colors (character vector, default c("#E41A1C", "#377EB8", "#4DAF4A", "#984EA3")) Border colors taxa unique facet level diff_contour mode. Recycled shorter number facet levels. level gets distinct color unique taxa different groups visually distinguishable. diff_border_width (numeric, default 1.5) Border width used diff_contour mode. show_title (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, adds informative title describing fill color, contour color, circle size, labels represent. match_by (character, default "refseq") match taxa physeq list_phyloseq. Passed merge_lpq(). One "refseq" (match reference sequences) "names" (match taxa names).

what: Scatter plots of Hill diversity across pairs of phyloseq objects — gg_hill_lpq
title: Scatter plots of Hill diversity across pairs of phyloseq objects — gg_hill_lpq
text: pair phyloseq objects list_phyloseq, computes Hill numbers (richness, Shannon exponential, inverse Simpson) per sample draws scatter plots comparing diversity samples across objects. visual diagnostic REPRODUCIBILITY, ROBUSTNESS, REPLICABILITY comparisons. point represents one sample. 1:1 reference line optional regression line help assess similar (different) diversity estimates two pipelines, runs, primers.

title: Scatter plots of Hill diversity across pairs of phyloseq objects — gg_hill_lpq
code: gg_hill_lpq(   x,   hill_scales = c(0, 1, 2),   pairs = NULL,   add_1to1 = TRUE,   add_smooth = TRUE,   cor_method = "pearson",   point_color = "black",   point_alpha = 0.6,   point_size = 2,   smooth_color = "steelblue",   verbose = TRUE )

title: Scatter plots of Hill diversity across pairs of phyloseq objects — gg_hill_lpq
text: x (required) list_phyloseq object. hill_scales (numeric vector, default c(0, 1, 2)) Hill number orders compute: 0 = richness, 1 = Shannon exponential, 2 = inverse Simpson. pairs (list integer pairs NULL, default NULL) pairs phyloseq objects compare. element must length-2 integer vector indices. NULL, pairwise combinations used. add_1to1 (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, adds dashed 1:1 identity line panel. add_smooth (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, adds linear regression line confidence interval via ggplot2::geom_smooth(). cor_method (character, default "pearson") Correlation method annotation label. One "pearson", "spearman", "kendall". point_color (character, default "black") Color scatter points. point_alpha (numeric, default 0.6) Transparency scatter points. point_size (numeric, default 2) Size scatter points. smooth_color (character, default "steelblue") Color regression line confidence ribbon. verbose (logical, default TRUE) TRUE, print message common samples used instead samples.

title: Scatter plots of Hill diversity across pairs of phyloseq objects — gg_hill_lpq
text: ggplot2::ggplot() object panels arranged grid: rows = pairs phyloseq objects, columns = Hill number orders.

title: Scatter plots of Hill diversity across pairs of phyloseq objects — gg_hill_lpq
text: function works common samples across phyloseq objects. list_phyloseq same_samples = TRUE, samples used. samples differ (e.g., NESTED_ROBUSTNESS), intersection kept. Comparison type context: REPRODUCIBILITY High correlation expected Hill orders. ROBUSTNESS Moderate high correlation desirable; deviations indicate pipeline sensitivity. REPLICABILITY Correlation may vary across Hill orders depending primer technology differences.

title: Scatter plots of Hill diversity across pairs of phyloseq objects — gg_hill_lpq

Using 137 common samples. #> Taxa are now in columns.  gg_hill_lpq(lpq, hill_scales = c(0, 1)) #> Samples differ across phyloseq objects.
